speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of an interview with Robert Ness on August the 24th, 1995. So from Suwalki when the war started, you then returned to your home town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Returned to-- you returned to Raczki.

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the Germans were already there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were there and then they left. They would stay there again for a few days, and then they left. And suddenly, lo and behold, by around-- by the end of September, the Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the country had been divided.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Russians came in the 17s. They-- they entered Poland from the east. That's what they call the liberation of Western-- Eastern-- Western Ukraine for them, and Western Belorussia. But really, they-- they just took-- partitioned it with Hitler together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's attitude, do you recall, towards the Russians before they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- towards the Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea, no idea. I know that Mother knew Russian, and my aunt was teaching us some of this rhyme, this nursery in Russian. There was some other Russian songs that-- from the nursery, and-- I-- I-- I don't really know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were happy to see the Russian, I'm sure of that, because as soon as the Russians came, when-- then when the agreement was, and the partition was made, our little town became Germany, German part, and Augustow became the Russian part. And it's-- from what my sister tells me, that we are just about the only families that went with the German-- with the Russians to Augustow. And we left that little town, and the Russians-- the Russians stayed there there, I think, for three days, three or four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Raczki with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Russians. And that's the last time I saw Raczki. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I presume Father, he-- he must have known what goes on in the world with Hitler and with the Germans. It's not the same Germans as the Germans from the First World War. And he already knew about not going to Hitler Germany, and not do-- doing business with them. And the fact that he was arrest-- or not he. I think it was his younger brother. Wasn't him, but younger brother Shmuel, that was supposedly arrested by the Germans as a spy, and my grandfather was told about it. And he had a heart attack and he died. It was about 1916. And it wasn't my father. It was a-- after talking to my sister, he told me it was Sam, who later moved to Argentina, then to the United States. And he was one of the uncles that we came to after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because of this incident in World War I, your father had-- there was a negative impression of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- well, he must have known what goes on with Hitler, with-- with-- in the German, what goes on in Germany. And he must have known-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Obviously, he was aware of it. So I presume that he figures that certainly we go with the Russians and get out. And so we even managed to get a lot of our merchandise too. It's my understanding that my-- when the war started, my father and my sister, Pola, went with a lot of the merchandise, textile. And you know textile at that point, now you are really-- it's-- it's like gold. They went to Bakirova, a small little town, and came back. And then the Russians came. And then when the Russians left, we left with them. And we came to Augustow. But my aunt was still in Raczki, the one-- Sarah, the one-- my father's sister, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who lived with us. And it-- it's as crazy as can be. You know, there was a border already. You know, you had to go through guards. I mean, dangerous as can be to cross between the-- she used to come on Friday morning to help Mother prepare the Sabbath. I mean, it's as crazy as can be.  You couldn't believe it if you wouldn't have seen it yourself with your own eyes. And she did it twice. And the third time, she came, Father said, now that's it. OK, no more of this nonsense. And as a matter of fact, later on before they even took the Jews away from Suwalki and took them in November, in '41 to Biala Podlaska around, I think, Lublin type of neighborhoods. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took the Jews of Raczki, dumped them on the border between the-- between Raczki and Augustow. And they say, here. We don't want them. You can have them. And so they came in and the Russians took them, and they were in-- and that's how some of the people that were with us in Slonim from Raczki, that's how they wound up in Augustow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Augustow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a number of places. First, we lived in one place. It was a house. And we had the merchandise, so the Russians came in without. There was a line, buying everything in sight. Whether it's for clothes or whether it's for-- for dresses or for shirts, anything in sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of merchandise was sold. And that's how we lived during the war, that Father had the money that he preserved from this-- from the sales. And then we moved outside suddenly in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a real cold winter, as can be. I remember it was below 30 centigrade, which is-- I don't know, figure it out. Most probably it's more than below-- it's close to 30 in Fahrenheit, or close to it, or 20 or something. And one of the Russian officers, he wanted the house and so on. And so we had to move out. And we moved out. I remember the horses, you could see the-- the-- the frozen whiskers on them. And when they were breathing, you could see like steam coming out of them. And we moved. And we moved to another place, to a party by the name of Demarcyzk. I don't think you need the spelling for it. But anyway, you'd say he was a Polish police officer in Raczki. Then he moved. He used to be a tenant of ours, where Grandma used to live in a house. When after she died, he stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we moved in, in his place. And that was on the way to Suwalki. There was a nice road, Raczki-- I mean Suwalki Augustow, which was asphalt and-- oh, it was-- it was already, aye, yi, yi, you know? Usually the roads weren't good, or just gravel, and-- Were you with school at the time at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was going in school. And it was a general school. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think it was even in Yiddish school at the time in Augustow. Might have been Russian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I did go to school, OK, there. And we stayed with the marching, and then at one time the Russians were picking up all the Polish police officers and others, and Father got wind of it. So he mentioned to the march again. He got over and went over to the German side and ran away from Augustow. My sister was going-- on that particular road, on the road to Suwalki there was a gymnasium in Augustow. So my sister Pola finished her schooling in Augustow, finished gymnasium. And that was in Polish. That was s Polish gymnasium, and she finished it. She was an excellent student and with honors, with all the other stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, as far as under-- under the Soviets, any persecution because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Listen. You get away from-- from Hitler in Germany, that was heaven, you know? No. None whatsoever. I was in Augustow, and then we moved because of the fact that we were refugees. And as refugees, we had to leave about 250 kilometers away from the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to move, but my father registered to go to Lithuania. Lithuania at that time was still a free country. There must have been rumors that you can still get out from Lithuania somehow. So my father registered to move to Lithuania. To try to then get--

speaker: interviewee
text: To try to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to United States, yes, or someplace. So it's-- and there was a local communist Jewish fellow who was, you know, more papally than the pope. And he was saying, no, no, there's nothing. Lithuania, it's-- doesn't take anything, that's it. You have to move out. So we moved to Suwalki-- I mean, to Slonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Further into the interior?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the interior, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Slonim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's just-- first of all, it must have been close to the limitation where we had to move away from because being refugees. As refugees, we couldn't live on the border. Had to be in the interior. And Slonim was far and away from Augustow. It was closer to most probably Minsk than to Augustow, I think. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Slonim, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Slonim-- we didn't come into Slonim in the city, because there was no room in the city. So we were about six kilometers from the city, [POLISH], a village. I used to go to school, walk six kilometers in the morning, and come back six kilometers, back morning and afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So you're talking four, five miles walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a Yiddish school. And it was a Yiddish school with the general subject in Yiddish, but you also had Russian, of course. And I had also German as a foreign language still. I mean, imagine for a Jewish school in those days to still have German as a foreign language. But anyway, that was-- that's what it was. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to-- now-- and then we stayed there for a few months, and then we moved on the outskirts of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Slonim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slonim, right, at Walenska 17 RA. That's where we lived. But that was a-- a house away from the road. In the spring, it was always flooded. The Shara, the-- the lake, and the river used to go out of-- out of its bounds, and used to flood. And we used to go from the corner. Somebody want to go home, you had to call the boats. Somebody, if they saw you, they-- they would come quicker. If not, you had to wait till a boat came to pick you up to go to the-- to this particular building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- was there any Jewish religious life at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I didn't go anymore to any synagogues, and no more the Saturday services, and no more Maftirs, and no more haftarahs. It was out. At that time I, was worried about being a Pioneer, to become a Pioneer, the Communist youth organization. The Pioneers, you know, Komsomol, and below that are Pioneers, and below that are the October children, you know. The youngsters. And I was worried whether or not I would be accepted become a Pioneer because my father was a merchant before the war, you know. It's almost like the enemy of the people. But I was. I did become a Pioneer, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in a-- it was a Communist youth organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a Communist youth movement, yes. I-- I was, at that time, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything at all of the-- about the activities in the movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember singing, learning Russian songs. I was pretty good at singing so I learned it fast. I remember discussions just about being good to the fatherland and then this, and patriotism and so forth. It was not much discussion of anything. That's all I remember. It's-- I remember too, we were going in the wintertime to go about 15 kilometer march on going on-- on-- on-- what would you call it-- on the snow, the-- you know. I'm getting a block.

speaker: interviewer
text: The skis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skis, that's it.  That's the word I was looking for. But I didn't go on skis. But I marched all right. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents still trying to emigrate at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what-- that was-- forget about emigrate. That was just-- that was the end of any emigration talk. Some people left voluntarily into deeper Russia from the refugees. And then later on, people were taken away. I remember going just before the war when they were taking-- were-- families were being evacuated, taken away. There were Jews, most of it were Poles. The Intelligentsia, I suppose. I didn't know what it was at the time.  I knew that I saw those box cars--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and people were around, and they were leaving the place. So I-- I saw some of the evacuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Enemies of the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Enemies of the people, so called.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably, sure, enemies of the people, Vrag naroda. Enemy of the people, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came June 22, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came June 22, and suddenly the talk that there was war with Germany, that Germany attacked Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any inkling that it-- war was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The days just before that, the trains were going west from Russia. That must have been part of the agreement that they had, where they used to supply the raw material to Hitler, and-- and there was-- we were living right next to the railroad station. So you could resolve to stop those heavy trains moving west, west, day and night, day and night. No. But I was-- oh, I was elated when the war was on. I figured, OK, now, it's going to be Russia too in the war, and really the Germany is going to get it. And I-- I thought it's-- it's-- it's time that now the-- the French and German, England is having an ally, and Germany is going to get it from both sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was absolutely happy as can be at the time, I remember it. I told you that during the time, I was already exposed to the newspapers. I used to run to stay in line to get the major paper. So I was way ahead of boy of 11, 12 years old, as far as knowledge, what goes on in the world, the wars, the positions of the armies.

speaker: interviewee
text: North Africa, I knew it like-- like my hand, as to whether it was Benghazi, Derna, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Trobruk, and then back and forth. You know, I was acquainted with that stuff. I-- I knew it like ad

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Germans enter Slonim?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- do you recall your-- your first impressions or some of their first actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: First impression was  we-- we had-- we build a-- a big hole at the-- kind of digged into the-- a big hole in the ground away from our house. Our house was on the river and not far from the electrical stations. So we were kind of-- and not far from the railroad. It had all the benefits of being close to military targets, what you might call.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we build a hole. And then we took out some stuff from the house, furniture. Not furniture so much as clothing and bedding and other stuff. And we used to stay there during the day time. The shooting was already coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly, we saw kind of a different uniforms crossing the street, running through it. And then the Germans were in town. And then we saw Russian prisoners. The next few days, we saw prisoners being taken. But we saw the Russians passing the river and trying to hide. And some of people did help them, and some didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: The soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers, right. So that was-- that's how I remember the German entering into the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember too the bombing of the railroad station. And I was right on Walenska Street, and by God, the Stukas were passing. And you could see those black crosses right there. And I was under a tree next to the lake, and I could almost see the pilot. That's how low they were flying over the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first actions taken by the Germans against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that started-- it was July 15th. That's where they started to move the Jews out from certain section of the city. It was not quite a ghetto yet, but it was already that these streets are out. You are in a ghetto. You are not allowed to live in those districts. And the districts, you're not allowed to live in those districts. So it became smaller and smaller, and before you know it, the ghetto was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were these orders communicated? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they used to have what they called the [GERMAN], which is giving a notice just on the walls. And people were told that you-- the Jews have to move out from this streets for three hours a day or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when was the-- the ghetto established?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say its-- the start is July 15, but most probably [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe at the end of July, as far as since the movement of the people. But official, I would say it was July 15th when it started. It started to restrictions, and then it became already a ghetto. And that-- the-- the Polish population or the white Russian population was out of the places as it moved on further. So then it was only Jews. So I would say before July 15th, until by the end of the month it was all Jewish quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change once you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't change yet too much, you know. It wasn't-- there wasn't wires yet separately and apart. Even so there were already some wires in some places. Yes, there was. Because in November already when the first slaughter of the Jews took place, there was already segregated with wires. And-- but it wasn't-- as when I was later in the ghettos, you know, with guards and-- it-- it wasn't that nature yet. It was still wires. There was a main gate that you had to go in and out. But not even '41 year, there was no gates and entry. That is the entry where you enter. It was in the later one that it was already had a main gate, and the wires were all around, and guards were already walking around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't even too much guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were separated already. Around that time when the first slaughter started November 14th.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. They had a ghetto within a ghetto. So they had a workers quarter where they gave people what they called a schein, which is like a certificate or something, a little card saying, let's say-- my father didn't have it, but let's assume that if you would have had it, it would have been written Hirsch Nussbaum and four people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened was many people who worked-- so a brother, suddenly his sister was his wife. Or if he was a little bit older, his mother was his wife, and that was his daughter. I mean, people were trying to be on the list. And I was, at that time-- our friend from Raczki-- Ratzki is the Yiddish version of it-- was in one of those quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went over there to stay with them already, because it sounded like that that is a safer place, that they might not touch on because these are people working for the Germans. They have certificates. And it's separated already from the other ghettos. My father was in hiding outside of the ghetto at some people that he knew on the-- on one side of the street was the Jewish ghetto, and the other side was not, was outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Slonim.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Slonim, Pushkinskaya, Pushkin at the time. And my sisters, too, were from some friends that we knew through then our landlord. Our landlord was not a Jew. He was white Russian. Through his family. So they went there, and I went to the Marianski's. And you know, it was a safe place at that particular day. And somehow or other, I figured, oh, I have to go and tell it to my father. He shouldn't come back to the ghetto. So I go ahead through the park, go outside, move over on the other side. And bingo, I started to walk where my father was hiding. It wasn't necessary it would have been that particular day. I could have stayed in this particular quarter, and it would have been the end of it. But I went there. Now suddenly I'm outside of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came there, and the woman got suddenly scared. And she didn't want to keep, and-- so I said look, I will go. And Father stayed. So at least-- and then I went back, and I figure where should I go? And I figured I'll go to my old place of Walenska Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had to cross the river and a guard. I had a warm coat on, and I had a handkerchief. I put my handkerchief around my head as if I have a toothache. I was alert on that somehow or other. And I come up to the guard, to him on the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to pass. And he said, where are you going? I say, I am going to the dentist. I have a headache. And he said, that's not a day to go to dentist. Go back to your [GERMAN]. Go back under the-- under the feather bed at your mother's.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] He was a local one from the white Russians that was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now this-- this was happening--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's November 14th--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around approximately 9 o'clock in the morning. I am already-- I left the quarter. I went to my father where he was hiding. I passed him. I went the other street. I passed it. There was a Polish school with kind of problems a little bit. I mean, a school of-- outside of the ghetto. But I passed that one. And suddenly, I didn't know where to go. So I turned back. And I had a friend of mine who was living outside the ghetto not far from the post office, because some officers were quartered in their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go there to knock on-- I started to knock on the door. Nobody answered. I knock again. Nobody answered. And suddenly, I see a German policeman and two locals are walking on the main street turning toward the post office. So I figure they're going to come there anyhow. So I started to go towards them. And they started, where are you going? I said, I am going to exchange stamps to-- for-- with the Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN]. I didn't-- I should have mentioned that before. One day there was a hanging supposedly of partisans. So I am there watching the big occasion, hanging of people, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next there was still the German, what you would call a Master Sergeant. And he was looking too, and I was looking. And I had stamps on me, a little booklet of stamps. So I kind of took it out as if I'm looking at the stamps. I really wanted to see-- to get his attention. Maybe he's a stamp collector or something. And sure enough, he was a stamp collector. And start saying, oh, Briefmarkensammeln, stamp collector. I said, yeah. And I started to talk to him. And he said, yeah, he's interested in stamp. And I showed him some of the stamps. And I made contact. That was already in must have been October, a good month before this November 14. I went already to him a few times. I brought him stamps, and he used to give me butter and bread. So I already established that I'm poor. And so I said that I am going to Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN], to bring him stamps, to exchange stamps. And I show them the stamps with me, which I had stamps with me. And didn't look like a Jewish boy. Keep in mind, I was a blond fellow with that-- with that cap that I showed you there, that we have. This was my cap I was wearing. I remember when I used to sometimes go and stay outside the ghetto. Just in case anybody thought maybe it's a Jewish boy, I used to take my finger and put it against the nose like that to make it so up nose that really nobody would think it's a Jewish boy. And what, a young boy is digging his nose, so he's playing with his nose, and that was all. But anyway, so they saw I have stamps. I told them where this Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN] is, that he's in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: This Master Sergeant?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: This Master Sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Master Sergeant, right. And I went in the place to the-- where used to be the-- the Russian school during 1940, '41, OK? And I passed there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come there. It-- it-- it's quiet suddenly, and an unteroffizier-- which would be a sergeant-- walks out. And what are you doing here? And [GERMAN]. I don't-- I don't-- I can tell it in German, but it's basically it goes, what are you doing here, or get out of here. I say, oh, I came to Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN]. And I give him the same spiel over again. I came to Oberfeldwebel to see him and exchange for the stamps. And yep, I go back to my stamps to show him. He said, that's not a day for stamp collecting. But you see, Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN] is above him, so he was a little bit-- couldn't quite say, you know-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stop now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then he walked away after bawling me out and this and that. But I still stayed there. I took-- kind of stood my ground, in a sense. And I didn't know where to turn, so I went ahead and I went to the outhouse. There was an outhouse for the soldiers. And I went into one, and I closed myself in. And I was sitting there and sitting there, and somebody used to come and knock on the outhouse to get in. When-- but then somebody, they got suspicious. Before you know it, two, three German soldiers come in, and knock, knock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I open the outhouse. Oh, is master here? What are you doing here, and what is that? So I started the same spiel all over again. I said, yeah. Oberfeldwebel [GERMAN]. And I have my stamps, and I came to see him. And he's not here yet, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bought it. And I was in the place. And then somebody told me, OK, go on the second floor. And I went up there, and they put me in a room. Put me in a room. And there were two soldiers sitting in the-- at a table. And behind them, as you entered it on the left side was those furniture type of things like you had in Europe, where you put in hanging the clothes in the closet, but it's not the closet with inside the walls, you know. And-- But I wasn't-- I was sitting with them at the table, and suddenly a young soldier comes in. And I thought, you know, the younger ones is usually maybe more Nazi inclined and so on. And he came and he said, it's terrible [SPEAKING GERMAN], and it's terrible what they are doing to the Jews there. I was as shocked as can be, you know.
